tech.summary: Agrivoltaics integrates solar panels with farming, addressing land-use conflicts by enabling simultaneous crop production and electricity generation. This dual-purpose approach allows farms to maintain agricultural output while panels generate power, creating a sustainable synergy between food and energy production.

learn.title: Solar PV: Photoelectric Effect in Panels
learn.intro: Solar photovoltaic panels transform sunlight into electricity, a cornerstone of global renewable energy growth. Understanding their operation helps explain how clean power scales to meet modern demands, reducing fossil fuel dependence and combating climate change through abundant sunlight harvesting.
learn.c1_text: When sunlight hits a solar cell, photons energize silicon atoms, freeing electrons. This photoelectric effect converts light directly to electricity without moving parts. Silicon's semiconductor properties enable controlled electron flow, similar to a dam releasing water when sunlight provides the energy pressure.
learn.c2_text: Metal contacts on the solar cell collect freed electrons, creating a directional current. The p-n junction (silicon layers doped with impurities) ensures electrons flow from positive to negative, generating DC electricity. This current powers homes or feeds into grids via inverters converting DC to AC.
learn.c3_text: India's solar capacity reached 67 GW (2023), targeting 100 GW by 2022 under MNRE initiatives. Bihar's 1 MW solar pump project demonstrates rural electrification using photoelectric technology. Rooftop solar subsidies under KUSUM scheme incentivize farmer-owned solar pumps, reducing diesel dependence in agriculture.
learn.stat2: Single panel produces 300-400 W under STC
learn.stat3: Silicon absorbs photons with energy >1.1 eV

learn.title: Wind Turbines: Aerodynamics & Generators
learn.intro: Wind turbines harness kinetic energy from moving air, converting it to electricity through precise engineering. Understanding their aerodynamics and generator mechanics reveals how these structures efficiently generate power, supporting global decarbonization as wind becomes the fastest-growing energy source worldwide.
learn.c1_text: Turbine blades act like airplane wings, creating lift as wind flows over curved surfaces. This aerodynamic design converts 80% of wind's kinetic energy into rotational motion. Modern blades span 40-80m, with optimized shapes maximizing energy capture even in low wind speeds (3-5 m/s threshold).
learn.c2_text: Rotational force spins a generator containing magnets and coils, inducing electric current via electromagnetic induction. Variable-speed turbines adjust RPM to wind speed, maintaining optimal efficiency. Power electronics condition output for grid compatibility, with typical turbines generating 2-3 MW per unit at 15-20 rpm rotor speed.
learn.c3_text: India's wind capacity reached 45 GW (2023), concentrated in Tamil Nadu (14 GW), Gujarat (8 GW), and Maharashtra (5 GW). Coastal and hill regions with average wind speeds >7 m/s enable 25-30% capacity factors. Green energy corridors facilitate transmission to demand centers, while offshore wind potential exceeds 300 GW.

learn.title: India's Renewable Purchase Obligation
learn.intro: India's Renewable Purchase Obligation mandates utilities to source power from clean energy, accelerating the country's 500 GW non-fossil capacity target by 2030. This policy framework drives investment in solar and wind projects while ensuring DISCOMs meet sustainability commitments through structured compliance mechanisms.
learn.c1_text: State DISCOMs must purchase 20-80% renewable energy based on state-specific targets. Non-compliance attracts penalties via CERC regulations. States like Tamil Nadu (80% RPO) and Rajasthan (70% RPO) lead implementation, while others like Haryana (30% RPO) show gradual adoption, creating structured demand for renewable generators.
learn.c2_text: DISCOMs fulfill RPO via utility-scale solar parks, wind farms, or Renewable Energy Certificates (RECs). Tamil Nadu's 1,000 MW solar park in Chennai and Gujarat's 1,600 MW wind corridor exemplify large-scale compliance. RECs allow trading, enabling states to meet targets through purchased certificates when direct procurement is challenging.
learn.c3_title: Policy Impact on Indian RE Sector 🇮🇳
learn.c3_text: RPO drove 2023's 18.7 GW solar and 4.5 GW wind additions. Karnataka's 40% RPO target increased solar tariffs by 15% in 2022. MNRE's 2023 policy revision allows hybrid renewable projects (solar+wind+battery), enhancing grid flexibility. States like Odisha offer 100% capital subsidy for RPO-compliant rooftop solar under Saurajya initiative.

learn.title: Bifacial Solar Panels: Dual-Side Power
learn.intro: Bifacial solar panels generate electricity from both front and rear surfaces, boosting energy yield through reflected sunlight (albedo effect). This innovation maximizes solar farm efficiency, particularly in high-reflectivity environments, supporting India's ambitious renewable targets while reducing land use requirements through enhanced power density.
learn.c1_text: Bifacial panels capture direct sunlight on the front and reflected light on the rear via transparent backsheet or glass-glass construction. Albedo values (0.2-0.9) determine rear-side gains—snow (0.8) and sand (0.4) provide significant boost. Yield increases 10-30% depending on installation height and ground reflectivity.
learn.c2_text: Higher power output (400-550 W) comes from dual-junction cells and reduced hot-spot losses. Elevated mounting (1-2m) optimizes albedo capture. Lower degradation rates (0.3%/year vs 0.5%) extend lifespan. Compatible with single-axis trackers, they excel in arid regions like Rajasthan where ground reflection is substantial.
learn.c3_text: India's 1,000 MW Kurnool Ultra Mega Solar Park (AP) uses bifacial panels, achieving 25% higher yield than monofacial. Gujarat's 500 MW Pavagada project incorporates bifacial technology with elevated structures. MNRE's 2023 subsidy scheme for bifacial panels in high-albedo regions encourages adoption in desert states, with Tamil Nadu leading pilot projects.

learn.title: EV Charging Levels: L1, L2, DC Fast
learn.intro: Electric vehicle charging infrastructure spans three levels with vastly different speeds and applications, critical for India's 300,000+ EV sales growth in 2023. Understanding Level 1 (home), Level 2 (public), and DC fast charging enables strategic infrastructure planning for seamless EV adoption across residential, commercial, and highway networks.
learn.c1_text: Level 1 uses 120V outlets (2-5 kW), adding 3-5 miles range per hour. A full charge takes 8-20 hours for most EVs. Ideal for overnight home charging with existing infrastructure. India's 2023 policy mandates 1 EV charger per 5 parking spaces in new commercial buildings, prioritizing Level 1 accessibility.
learn.c2_text: Level 2 (240V, 7-22 kW) charges 25-50 miles/hour—full charge in 4-8 hours. Found in workplaces and public stations. DC fast chargers (50-350 kW) deliver 100-400 miles in 15-30 minutes, enabling highway travel. India's 2023 National EV Policy targets 10,000 DC fast chargers by 2030.
learn.c3_text: India had 12,000+ EV chargers (Dec 2023), concentrated in Delhi-NCR (3,000), Mumbai (2,500), and Bengaluru (1,800). NH Charging Corridor project installs DC fast chargers every 100 km on national highways. State subsidies under FAME II support workplace Level 2 deployment, with Gujarat leading in solar-powered charging stations.

learn.title: Hydrogen Fuel Cells: Clean Vehicle Power
learn.intro: Hydrogen fuel cells generate electricity through electrochemical reactions, producing only water as a byproduct—ideal for heavy transport and industrial applications. As India targets 5% hydrogen blending in CNG by 2030, understanding fuel cell technology reveals pathways for decarbonizing sectors difficult to electrify directly, supporting net-zero ambitions.
learn.c1_text: Fuel cells combine hydrogen (H₂) and oxygen (O₂) at separate electrodes via a PEM membrane. Hydrogen splits into protons and electrons; protons pass through membrane, electrons flow through external circuit creating electricity. Heat and water are the only outputs. Efficiency reaches 60% (vs 30% for combustion), with operating temps of 60-80°C.
learn.c2_text: Fuel cell electric vehicles (FCEVs) refuel in 3-5 minutes, offering 300-500 mile ranges. Ideal for buses, trucks, and trains where battery weight limits practicality. Power density exceeds batteries (1,000 Wh/kg vs 250 Wh/kg). India's 2023 hydrogen roadmap targets 100 fuel cell buses in Delhi and 500 trucks in logistics corridors by 2030.
learn.c3_text: India's National Green Hydrogen Mission allocates $2.8 billion (2023) for electrolyzer manufacturing and pilot projects. Gujarat's 800 MW green hydrogen plant (2024) and Haryana's 100-ton/day ammonia project exemplify scale-up. MNRE's hydrogen blending policy supports CNG infrastructure, with 10 hydrogen refueling stations planned in Delhi-NCR by 2025.
